title: Nanaimo bars
subtitle: Canadian no-bake chocolate and custard layered squares
excerpt: Nanaimo bars are a Canadian confection in three distinct layers: a chocolate, coconut, and biscuit base cooked briefly with egg to set firm; a thick, pale custard buttercream middle; and a thin dark chocolate top. They require no oven; the base is heated on the hob just long enough to cook the egg. They are sliced into small squares from the fridge and eaten cold. The custard layer is what sets them apart from a standard chocolate fridge cake.
sourceNotes: Nanaimo bars are named after Nanaimo, British Columbia, and have been associated with the city since at least the 1950s. The first printed recipe appears in the Nanaimo Hospital Auxiliary cookbook (1952). The custard middle layer, made with custard powder, distinguishes the Canadian version from similar American no-bake bars. The bars were entered in a 1985 competition run by the city of Nanaimo, with the winning recipe published in local food guides.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 square tin (20 × 20 cm), cuts into 16 to 25 pieces
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in an airtight container between layers of parchment. Slice from frozen with a hot knife.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps refrigerated for up to 1 week. The chocolate top cracks cleanly when cold.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; base is cooked briefly over low heat on the hob

content.text: The base requires care: the butter, sugar, cocoa, and egg are melted together over a very low heat, stirring constantly. The egg cooks into the mixture rather than scrambling; the target is a slightly thickened, glossy paste that coats the spoon. If the heat is too high, the egg scrambles and the base is grainy. Stir without stopping and remove from the heat the moment the mixture thickens.

Line a 20 × 20 cm tin with parchment. Melt 120 g butter with 50 g sugar and 30 g cocoa in a saucepan over very low heat, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and leave for 1 minute to cool slightly. Stir in the beaten egg continuously until the mixture thickens and becomes glossy; about 1 minute. Work quickly so the egg does not scramble. Stir in 200 g crushed digestive biscuits, 60 g desiccated coconut, and 50 g walnuts if using. Press firmly and evenly into the lined tin. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Beat 80 g softened butter with 30 g custard powder until combined. Beat in 250 g sifted icing sugar and 30 ml milk until smooth and pale. Spread evenly over the chilled base. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until firm.
Add the chocolate top and chill

Melt 150 g dark chocolate with 15 g butter over a bain-marie or in short microwave bursts until smooth. Cool to about 35 degrees C; it should be fluid but not hot. Pour over the custard layer and tilt the tin to spread evenly. Refrigerate for at least 90 minutes until completely set. To slice: remove from the tin, score the chocolate top lightly first, then cut through with a sharp knife dipped in hot water.

items.problem: Base has a grainy, scrambled-egg texture
items.solution: The heat was too high when the egg was added. Use the very lowest heat, remove the pan from the burner first, wait a minute for it to cool slightly, then add the beaten egg while stirring without stopping.

items.problem: Chocolate top cracked badly when sliced
items.solution: The bars were too cold. Take them out of the fridge 10 minutes before slicing. Dip the knife in hot water and wipe it between cuts. Scoring the chocolate lightly before cutting through completely reduces cracking.

items.problem: Custard middle is very sweet and hard
items.solution: Reduce the icing sugar by 30 g and add 1 extra teaspoon of milk to loosen slightly. The custard layer should be firm but yield easily to biting.
